data.title: S&P 500, Nasdaq notch record closes as retail sales and earnings fuel rally
data.description:
Wall Street closed out trading higher on Thursday, with both the Nasdaq Composite and S&P 500 surging to fresh record highs as investors weighed new data on retail sales and jobless claims.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended on top by 0.5% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) had climbed up by 0.5%. At the same time, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was able to advance by 0.7% on the session.

Among the 11 S&P 500 sectors, nine ended in the green, with Financials and Info Tech at the top of the leaderboard. Going the other way was Health Care, as the segment was the weakest perfomer on the day.

U.S. Treasury yields observed limited moves as the shorter-end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) gained 2 basis points to 3.92% and the longer-end U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) had risen 1 basis point to 4.46%. 

Looking out at the economic calendar and June retail sales came in at +0.6% M/M to $720.1B vs. +0.1% consensus and -0.9% prior (unrevised), while core retail sales, excluding motor vehicles and parts, came in at +0.5% M/M vs. +0.3% consensus and -0.6% prior (revised from -0.3%). 

“Strong June retail sales show consumers may have been spooked by tariffs, but they haven't fully gone into hiding. The fact that June is a slower month and that goods outlays comprise a fraction of overall outlays means that we are not completely rethinking our forecast for a soft second half for consumer spending,” Wells Fargo’s Tim Quinlan said. 

“Falling unemployment claims and good retail numbers suggest the economy is just chugging along, taking away fears of an overheating economy that won’t allow the Fed to cut, and fears about a potential tariff-induced recession,” Seeking Alpha analyst Leo Nelissen stated.

“While it’s fair to say the S&P 500 is expensive at 22x forward earnings, it won’t matter until investors start to doubt the growth story. Today was not that day,” Nelissen added.

Furthermore, initial jobless claims unexpectedly fell in the past week. Business inventories also held steady again in May at 0.0% M/M to $2,656.7B. Additionally, homebuilder sentiment ticked up one point in July, as was expected. 

“The VIX eased slightly overnight. Despite the back-and-forth headlines concerning the possible sacking of Fed Chair Powell and President Trump’s trade wars, market participants ultimately didn’t panic, leaving implied volatility apparently well anchored. The muted reaction to yesterday’s events suggests that traders remain focused on earnings and macro data rather than political theatre, at least for now,” David Morrison of Trade Nation said. 

Earnings remain in focus, with Travelers Corp, Citizens Financial and US Bancorp reporting. Netflix will headline after hours, offering a tech-sector litmus test amid rising subscriber growth expectations, Morrison added.

data.title: Interactive Brokers Group declares $0.08 dividend
data.description:
• Interactive Brokers Group (NASDAQ:IBKR) declared $0.08/share quarterly dividend, vs. prior dividend of $0.32.
  • Prior period share and per share amounts have been retroactively adjusted to reflect the four-for-one forward stock split, effected in the form of a stock dividend, on June 17, 2025.
  • Forward yield 0.54%
  • Payable Sept. 12; for shareholders of record Sept. 1; ex-div Sept. 1.
  • See IBKR Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.

data.title: Interactive Brokers Group Non-GAAP EPS of $0.51 beats by $0.04, revenue of $1.48B beats by $80M
data.description:
• Interactive Brokers Group press release (NASDAQ:IBKR): Q2 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.51 beats by $0.04.
  • Revenue of $1.48B (+20.3% Y/Y) beats by $80M.
  • Customer accounts increased 32% to 3.87 million.
  • Customer equity increased 34% to $664.6 billion.
  • Total DARTs increased 49% to 3.55 million.
  • Customer credits increased 34% to $143.7 billion.
  • Customer margin loans increased 18% to $65.1 billion.
  • Shares +1.97% AH.

data.title: Netflix GAAP EPS of $7.19 beats by $0.12, raises FY25 revenue guidance
data.description:
• Netflix press release (NASDAQ:NFLX): Q2 GAAP EPS of $7.19 beats by $0.12.
  • Revenue of $11.08B (+15.9% Y/Y) beats by $20M.
  • Q2 revenue increased by 16% year over year (17% on a foreign exchange (F/X) neutral basis ). Year-over-year revenue growth was primarily a function of more members, higher subscription pricing and increased ad revenue. All regions experienced healthy year-over-year revenue growth, with each region posting double-digit F/X neutral increases. UCAN revenue growth accelerated year over year to 15% vs. 9% in Q1’25 due to the full quarter impact of price changes.
  • The company now forecast 2025 revenue of $44.8-$45.2B, up from $43.5-$44.5B vs $44.5B consensus, and a F/X neutral operating margin of 29.5% (vs. 29% previously), or 30% on a reported basis. Our higher forecast primarily reflects the weakening of the US dollar vs. most other currencies, plus healthy member growth and ad sales.

  • Guidance:

    Q3 Revenue of $11.53B vs $11.28B consensus.

    EPS of $6.87 vs $6.71 consensus.

  • Shares +1%.

data.title: FDA announces recall of certain Dexcom glucose monitoring devices
data.description:
The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has announced a recall of specific Dexcom (NASDAQ:DXCM) glucose monitoring devices, including the G6, G7, ONE, and ONE+ models.

The recall is due to a potential issue with the speaker that may prevent the devices from sounding an alert when blood sugar levels are critically low or high.This recall is categorized as the most serious type, as using the affected devices could lead to severe health risks, such as seizures, vomiting, loss of consciousness, or even death.According to the FDA, there have been at least 56 reported injuries linked to this issue, but no fatalities have been reported so far.The Dexcom G6, G7, ONE, and ONE+ devices are integral components of continuous glucose monitoring systems, which assist individuals with diabetes in monitoring their glucose levels in real time.

data.title: Ex-MPT tenant Steward cleared to liquidate after hospital selloff - report
data.description:
Steward Health Care System, the former tenant of Medical Properties Trust (NYSE:MPW), won approval to liquidate in bankruptcy court, according to a recent media report.

Steward's liquidation plan was approved by U.S. Bankruptcy Judge Christopher Lopez, the Wall Street Journal reported. Amid spiraling financial and operational distress, Steward offloaded, shuttered or transitioned all 31 of its hospitals to new operators last year.

As part of the liquidation plan, Steward's top lenders are putting up $127M to fund a wave of litigation targeting the company's former insiders, counterparties and affiliates, the Journal said. Court papers show that Steward owes around $270M for professional fees.

Steward's bankruptcy estate has managed to raise about $600M by selling off roughly half of its hospitals, the article said, though the proceeds fell short of covering what's owed to secured lenders and the vendors who kept operations running during the Chapter 11 process.

Steward -- which still owes MPT (NYSE:MPW) for unpaid lease payments -- claimed its plan could ultimately recover as much as $800M through clawback lawsuits targeting insurers, ex-executives, among others. But doing so would push payments to top-priority creditors out by at least two years. 

In the meantime, Steward was said to have proposed distributing just $12M to administrative creditors, despite those claims totaling over $90M.

data.title: Goldman Sachs to purchase Santander loans worth €460M
data.description:
Goldman Sachs (NYSE:GS) has agreed to purchase about €460 million ($534 million) worth of Spanish mortgages from Banco Santander SA (NYSE:SAN), according to Bloomberg, which cited sources familiar with the deal.

The banking giant is acquiring the loans alongside other investors, including Pacific Investment Management Co., the report said.

Santander’s sale of performing and re-performing mortgages to Goldman Sachs and Pimco is part of its wider strategy to shed €40 billion ($46.39 billion) in risk-weighted assets by 2025. The move is aimed at shifting towards a leaner balance sheet in line with European regulators’ calls for stronger capital buffers and lower risk exposure.

data.title: Amicus gains as Morgan Stanley upgrades on Aurobindo case and Pom-Op switching
data.description:
Amicus Therapeutics (NASDAQ:FOLD) traded higher on Thursday after Morgan Stanley upgraded the stock, citing a favorable setup for the company ahead of a key ruling in its patent dispute with generic drug maker Aurobindo, as well as the potential for its Pompe disease therapy, Pom-Op.

Morgan Stanley upgraded Amicus FOLD to Overweight from Equal Weight and kept its price target unchanged at 12%, implying nearly 100% from the stock’s close on Wednesday.

The firm cited a favorable stance for the company heading into a summary judgment in the case with Aurobindo, against which Amicus (NASDAQ:FOLD) filed a lawsuit in 2022 over the alleged infringement of its Orange Book-listed patents related to its Fabry disease therapy, Galafold.

Additionally, Morgan Stanley cited long-term tailwinds from patients switching to the company’s new Pompe disease therapy, Pom-Op, also known as Pombiliti + Opfolda, from rival treatments, Lumizyme and Nexviazyme, from Sanofi (SNY).

data.title: First Industrial narrows 2025 FFO guidance to $2.88-$2.96 per share while signaling occupancy stability and development lease milestones
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. (FR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Peter E. Baccile stated the portfolio "continues to perform well, producing strong cash rental rate growth with a solid pace of renewals" and highlighted that "tenant leasing activity and investments to support new growth continue to move at a deliberate pace." He noted ongoing uncertainty around tariffs is "dampening momentum around decision-making," but pointed out new development leases, particularly the full lease-up of the 1.8 million square foot Camelback 303 project in Phoenix and a 58,000 square foot lease at First Loop in Orlando.
  • Baccile reported "in-service occupancy at quarter end was 94.2%, in line with our expectations, reflecting the known 708,000 square-foot move-out in Central Pennsylvania and the impact of two developments placed in service, partially offset by some new leasing."
  • The company achieved "overall cash rental rate increase for new and renewal leasing of 33%." Excluding a large fixed-rate renewal, the increase was 38%. Baccile confirmed expectations remain for "overall cash rental rate growth expectations of 30% to 40% and 35% to 45%, excluding the fixed-rate renewal."
  • New developments started include a 176,000 square foot facility in Northwest Dallas and a 226,000 square foot facility at First Park New Castle in Philadelphia, with a combined investment of $54 million and "target cash yields of approximately 8% for each."
  • Baccile highlighted a credit rating upgrade to BBB+ by Fitch and issuance of $450 million of senior unsecured notes at a 5.25% coupon.
  • CFO Scott A. Musil stated, "NAREIT funds from operations were $0.76 per fully diluted share compared to $0.66 per share in 2Q 2024. Our cash same-store NOI growth for the quarter, excluding termination fees, was 8.7%, primarily driven by increases in rental rates on new and renewal leasing and contractual rent bumps, partially offset by lower average occupancy."

Outlook

  • Musil reported, "Our guidance range for NAREIT FFO for the year remains $2.92 per share at the midpoint with the range narrowed to $2.88 to $2.96 per share. Our assumptions are as follows: average quarter-end in service occupancy of 95% to 96%."
  • The company expects "cash same-store NOI growth before termination fees of 6% to 7%." The guidance incorporates "approximately 1.5 million square feet of development leasing assumed to occur in the fourth quarter of this year."
  • Capitalized interest for the year is expected at about $0.09 per share, and G&A expense guidance is $40.5 million to $41.5 million.

Financial Results

  • NAREIT funds from operations were $0.76 per fully diluted share.
  • Cash same-store NOI growth for the quarter, excluding termination fees, was 8.7%.
  • In-service occupancy finished at 94.2%, reflecting both the move-out in Central Pennsylvania and new developments entering service.
  • Approximately 2.5 million square feet of leases commenced during the quarter, with 400,000 new leases, 2.1 million renewals, and 100,000 related to developments and acquisitions with lease-up.
  • The company issued $450 million of senior unsecured notes, maturing January 2031, at a coupon rate of 5.25%.

Q&A

  • Robert Chapman Stevenson, Janney: Asked about attractiveness of new development starts and construction pricing. Baccile responded that "to really get deeper into new starts, we'd like to see more consistent development lease signings," while Johannson L. Yap stated, "costs are down 5% to 10%, depending upon the market... from the start of the year, total construction cost has been pretty flat."
  • Stevenson asked about any non-recurring items in second quarter FFO. Musil replied, "in the third and fourth quarter, we're recognizing more interest expense than in the second quarter... that's about $110 million of spend for the last 6 months of the year... the May bond offering was slightly dilutive."
  • Vikram L. Malhotra, Mizuho: Asked about new leases and prospects for the 1.6 million square feet in guidance. Schultz identified Denver's First Aurora Commerce Center as the largest component and reported "active prospects for all or portions of the building."
  • Malhotra inquired about the $0.02 per share impact from lease-ups. Musil clarified, "with the benefit of that lease... that $0.02 a share is now $0.00 a share."
  • Nicholas Patrick Thillman, Baird: Asked about plans for Camelback 303. Yap stated, "the plan there is to... maximize value," and noted the JV project "delivered returns exceeding our expectations."
  • Craig Allen Mailman, Citi: Asked about lease-up timing for large vacancies. Baccile said it's "a demand side issue... It's really just finding the right fit at the right time for the particular candidate or potential tenant."
  • Blaine Matthew Heck, Wells Fargo: Asked about build-to-suit demand. Baccile replied, "returns are always going to be a little bit lower... our platform is set up and our land holdings are such that we're more targeted to the speculative development business."
  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about price concessions and bond issuance rationale. Baccile responded that "lowering the rate doesn't create new demand" and Musil noted bond issuance rationale was the company's path to be a "serial issuer in the public bond market."
  • Jessica Zheng, Green Street: Asked about private developers' behavior. Schultz noted, "where there are more choices, you've seen concessions drift up," and Yap added, "a lot of developers are sitting on their land because they've got still some vacancies."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on development leasing, the impact of tariffs, and the timing of lease-ups, with a slightly cautious tone, evidenced by questions about recurring FFO, leasing guidance, and demand-side challenges.
  • Management maintained a measured but confident stance in prepared remarks, with Baccile emphasizing "deliberate pace" and "expect improved confidence" once tariff clarity emerges. In Q&A, management was transparent about risks, with statements such as "the risk of getting those done is not 0" and "we'd like to see more consistent development lease signings."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts sustained a cautious but pragmatic tone around macro uncertainty, though management's confidence in long-term fundamentals remained steady.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for NAREIT FFO per share was revised to a narrower range of $2.88 to $2.96 from the prior $2.87 to $2.97, while the midpoint remained $2.92.
  • In-service occupancy decreased to 94.2% from 95.3% last quarter, reflecting the expected move-out in Central Pennsylvania and developments entering service.
  • Cash same-store NOI growth moderated to 8.7% from 10.1% in Q1, as lower average occupancy offset continued rental rate increases.
  • Analysts maintained focus on development leasing timelines, macro risks (notably tariffs), and the impact of new financing.
  • Management's tone remained constructive, with increased detail on development lease risks and cost trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "uncertainty around tariffs" as a continuing factor that "dampens momentum around decision-making."
  • Increased interest expense in the second half of the year due to continued funding of the development pipeline and higher bond coupon rates was flagged as a sequential drag on FFO.
  • Management acknowledged that some assets, such as the Denver First Aurora Commerce Center, face extended lease-up periods and that the risk of not leasing up certain space by year-end "is not 0."
  • Analysts expressed concern about occupancy and lease-up timing, as well as the impact of macroeconomic uncertainty on tenant decision-making.

Final Takeaway

First Industrial Realty Trust, Inc. emphasized the resilience of its operating portfolio, achieving strong rental rate growth and lease renewals despite headwinds from tariff uncertainties and lower occupancy. With substantial progress on development leasing and a full lease-up of major projects, management narrowed full-year FFO guidance and highlighted solid demand in target markets. The company remains focused on optimizing value through selective development and disciplined capital markets activity, while acknowledging external risks that may impact leasing velocity in the coming quarters.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
